prompt.content:
Use file “Onc_Patient 7” to access the entire medical history / record for the patient. Please use the patient tab as the source of truth for all demographic information. Disregard patient identifiers (Name, Age, DOB, MRN) found within the text of the clinicalnotes tab as they have been shifted for de-identification purposes

-----

Prompt: 
The patient presents to her oncologist to re-establish care after a few years. Refer to the reference files "Onc_Patient 7," "Onc_Patient 7 script.pdf," and “Labs.pdf”.

What is the next best systemic treatment option for this patient? What dosage is recommended for this medication? What frequency and route of administration are recommended for this medication? What is the most common reason for dose reduction? Provide a clinical rationale.

Answers should be in line with the most clinical guidelines as of January 13, 2026.

extra.task_name: Web Search_Attachments_Onc Patient 7 - EGFR2 exon 19 mutation - Eliane

golden_responses.golden_text_response:
1. What is the best systemic treatment option for this patient? 

The patient has a history of stage I non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) and previously received EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors and platinum-based chemotherapy. The disease is currently metastatic, with biopsy-proven recurrence of NSCLC and an EGFR exon 19 deletion mutation. The recommended next systemic therapy options for this patient are lazertinib plus amivantamab-vmjw.

2. What dosage is recommended for this medication? 
For a patient weighing 48.2 kg, the recommended dosing for lazertinib plus amivantamab is 240 mg lazertinib and 1050 mg amivantamab-vmjw.

3. What frequency and route of administration are recommended for this medication? 
Lazertinib is administered orally once daily.
Amivantamab-vmjw is administered intravenously weekly for the first 4 weeks, then every 2 weeks starting in cycle 2. 

5. What is the most common reason for dose reduction? 
The most common reason for dose reduction is dermatologic adverse reactions.
